data.title: National Beverage: Revenue Growth Headwinds Persist Through Q3 2026 And Beyond
data.description:
National Beverage Corp. (FIZZ) is currently facing a very tough environment on multiple sides. As of its third-quarter earnings of fiscal 2026 reported last week, revenue growth has declined sequentially for two quarters, both gross and operating expenses are showing upticks for the same period, and one finds some measure of weakness all the way to the bottom line.

These sequential declines are rarely highlighted in quarter earnings releases because the focus tends to be on year-on-year performance, so the market was exuberant for a short while but then gave it all back in a hurry.

One sees clearly how the stock initially gapped down on the night of the earnings call (March 12), but a bullish wave gave it two gaps up before it succumbed to bearish forces.

Regardless, the stock is now a little higher than what it was before earnings, so the other side of the dilemma would be this: the stock still trades at an average discount of more than 30 percent to its five-year multiples, its balance sheet is the strongest it has been in the last ten years ($314 million in CC&E), and the current ratio stands at 4.4, so no short-term liquidity issues, either.

This would seem to be an ideal value stock, but in order to confirm this, one needs to be certain that FIZZ's current revenue headwinds will not last longer than its liquidity can.

The Gist of Q3 2026 Performance

Declining revenues are usually the first signs of distress, but this must be seen in the context of the seasonal demand its beverages typically experience. The quarters spanning the winter months (Q3 and Q4) obviously see lower sell-throughs at retail outlets, but even if one strips out this seasonality, one sees that volumes are down across Power+ and carbonated. The downslide in revenue essentially came from the 4.4 percent price increase being unable to offset the 4.8 percent decline in case volumes.

COGS per case was up by 3.4 percent year-on-year but was more than offset by the price bump, resulting in a positive year-on-year trend for gross margins. However, one should not lose sight of the fact that the sequential decline is somewhat worrisome; even worse, Q4 2026 and Q1 2027 will be comped against two very strong quarters, when revenues came in at $313.6 million and $330.5 million, respectively. FIZZ may still achieve a year-on-year growth in gross margins, but pricing power will have diminished after the recent increases, so that is something to watch for over the next two quarters.

For the nine months ended January 31, 2026, the same pattern is visible - a 4.7 percent increase in pricing did not fully offset the 4.9 percent reduction in case volumes.

Consumer Perspective

By all measures, the average U.S. consumer is still resilient despite the higher prices. Per a report from Circana just this week:

During the combined four weeks ending February 28, 2026, macro retail trends across retail food and beverage, non-edible consumer packaged goods (CPG), and discretionary general merchandise show a return to generally steadier spending patterns, though demand is still being challenged by elevated prices. Retail food and beverage sales increased 1.4%, with units down just 0.6%.

"Steadier spending patterns" is good news for FIZZ and could translate to more stable case volumes for the coming quarters. They would still be comping strong quarters from the year-ago period, but consumers seem to be absorbing these price increases to a degree. More stability should help reduce the year-on-year volume declines, so this does not seem to be something to worry about in the near term.

Valuing the FIZZ Opportunity

In effect, what one has in one's hand with FIZZ is an operationally efficient business with a strong balance sheet and cash flows, but is this good value right now? We shall see.

The one Street analyst covering FIZZ does not seem too optimistic, with a revenue expectation of $296.4 million for Q4 2026 as of writing this, which is a sizeable decline from $313 million in the prior period. Extrapolate to a year forward, and one sees forward sales multiples on the rise compared to their TTM equivalents.

On the flip side, forward earnings multiples are slightly higher than trailing multiples because management is eking efficiency from what is now a declining revenue base.

This is not necessarily a bad position to be in from an investor's viewpoint, although management might disagree. A declining revenue base should indeed be their sole worry at this point, but for investors, the game is all about earnings and equity value both growing. That is certainly happening at FIZZ, albeit at a slower pace than its peers, so my inference is that the stock may keep getting cheaper while the decline at the top persists, but recovery will be strong when revenue growth rebounds. The question that remains is, "Will it?"

What Might Drive Revenue Growth?

First and foremost, a loosening of consumer purses is necessary to invigorate the consumer market, but in order for that to happen, interest rates need to come down. They have not, as we saw just this week with the decision of the FOMC to hold EFFRs steady for now. A comment by Standard ⁠Chartered's Steve Englander:

The hawkish part was the frustration Powell expressed at the slow pace of disinflation, very explicitly conditioning further policy rate cuts on inflation moving closer to target.

Reuters also reported that the Fed:

projected higher ​inflation, steady unemployment and a single reduction in borrowing costs this year, a path that Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said was subject to unusually high uncertainty as policymakers take stock ‌of the impact of the U.S. and Israeli war with Iran.

As opposed to 'usually high uncertainty,' perhaps? In any case, even this hint of "a single reduction" was wet-blanketed by this:

the Fed's next move "might be an increase did come up at the meeting as it did at the last meeting," Powell said, though he added that the "vast majority" of the officials don't have that outcome as their base case.

That does not mean they do not have it as their worst case.

This uncertainty makes it extremely difficult to forecast a trajectory for FIZZ, but I believe FIZZ has a key revenue driver that most companies would, at the very least, maim for: brand value.

It is not the intangibles in the book that I speak of. FIZZ holds less than $15 million in goodwill and intangibles combined, and that needle has not moved for at least ten years - as far back as Seeking Alpha takes me.

No, this is brand value like Apple (AAPL) commands in the market, with its zero percent goodwill and intangibles for that same period.

So where do these companies 'record' their actual brand value? Simple. The market cap less book value. Price to book value, or P/BV, in other words, and it is this market premium that quantifies brand value.

AAPL has an equity or book value of $88 billion but a market value of $3.73 trillion as I write this. The equity is just 2.4 percent of market value. Flip it, and one gets an over-40x premium in brand value.

FIZZ has an equity value of about $590 million and a market value of $3.24 billion, giving it 18.2 percent equity to market value. This is a much better deal, in my view, because the brand value premium is just 5.5x book value.

Okay, so an Apples-to-Peach-Strawberry-LaCroix comparison might not be the best one, but even within its own category, FIZZ is trading at a heavy discount to its peers.

What I find even more intriguing is how the P/B ratio has evolved over the past five years. Book value has grown the slowest of the lot, yet its P/B ratio has deteriorated by over 60 percent because the stock price is 30 percent down in the same period, compounding the slowness of equity growth.

This is where the growth drivers are embedded, ironically. A brand value premium of 5.5x is cheap when there are multiple market leaders and anchors in one's portfolio. FIZZ is a dominant brand in its categories. LaCroix is undoubtedly the crown-wearer in sparkling water; value pop brands like Shasta and Faygo have vice-like grips on their respective markets; and Rip It holds formidable sway over the value energy drink market. These are well-known facts based on real-world observations.

My Rating on FIZZ

Considering the unknowns of forward revenue growth, I cannot rate FIZZ a Buy just yet.

It is definitely deserving of a Hold rating because there is still that seasonal strength that allows FIZZ to keep reporting peak revenues in its growth quarters (Q1 and Q2). We shall have to bear with one more quarter (Q4) of declining year-on-year growth, but if new peaks are set in Q1 and Q2, we might well see a rerating of the stock closer to the P/B ratios of the incumbent beverage giants.

For me, this would turn into a Buy when the consumer economy starts to heat up again and revenue growth at FIZZ is once again positive. Since the visibility into those factors is limited at this time, I would only be comfortable rating this a Hold for now.

data.title: Watch 157.40 On USD/JPY, Hawkish BoJ, ECB And BoE Ignite Yen Strength
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

In the world of foreign exchange, we measured performance on a relative basis in terms of price action structures and macro factors.

A hawkish stance or guidance from the US Federal Reserve does not necessarily result in sustained US dollar strength, as the currency’s trajectory is ultimately shaped by relative monetary policy dynamics across other major developed market central banks.

The ex-post 18 March’s FOMC US dollar strength pop due to Fed funds futures market now pricing in no interest rate cuts by the Fed in 2026 was evaporated yesterday, reinforced by hawkish guidance from the European Central Bank (ECB) and the Bank of England (BoE) despite keeping their respective policy rates unchanged at 2% and 3.75%.

Ex-post FOMC US dollar strength evaporated

Interest swap markets in the eurozone and the UK have started to price in two 25 basis points (bps) hikes this year, each by the ECB and the BoE, due to their concerns on inflation risks arising from the slowdown driven by stagflation fear driven by the oil supply shock coming out from the Middle East (US-Iran War).

The US Dollar Index shed -1.1% on Thursday, March 19, 2026, erased the prior day's gain of 0.7% (ex-post FOMC), and the USD/JPY fared slightly worse off with a daily loss of 1.3%.

The Bank of Japan (BoJ) left its policy interest rate unchanged at 0.75%, and we have warned in our BoJ monetary preview report published earlier on Wednesday, March 18, that BoJ Governor Ueda’s press conference that tends to tilt towards dovish vibes more often based on past conferences is likely not to trigger a bout of strength in the USD/JPY this time around.

BoJ Ueda’s hawkish press conference

BoJ Governor Ueda highlighted in the post-monetary policy decision press conference that the current spring wage talks have been delivering high chances of another year of wage increases.

He also noted that authorities need to keep monitoring the impact of currency movements on consumer prices, as FX moves now may have more impact on prices than before.

These statements are considered hawkish. That suggests BoJ is still on the path of one interest rate hike before 2026 ends.

Let’s focus now on the short-term trajectory (1-3 days) of the USD/JPY from a technical analysis perspective.

USD/JPY – At risk of breaking below 20-day moving average

Watch the 159.03/159.37 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 157.50/157.40 (also the 20-day moving average) exposes the next intermediate support at 156.55 (also the 50-day moving average) in the first step.

On the flip side, a clearance above 159.37 invalidates the bearish bias for a squeeze up towards the next intermediate resistances at 160.23 and 160.74 (also the intervention risk zone where BoJ sold USD against JPY in the past).

Key elements to support the bearish bias on USD/JPY

  • The price actions of the USD/JPY have broken down below the minor ascending channel support from the February 27, 2026 low of 155.54.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has exited from the oversold region without any bullish divergence condition, which suggests that the current bounce from Thursday, 19 March 2026, low of 157.51 is a minor corrective rebound within a minor downtrend phase.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Allspring Absolute Return Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
GENERAL FUND INFORMATION

Ticker: WABIX

Portfolio managers:

Petros Bocray, CFA®, FRM;

Matthias Scheiber, CFA®, Ph.D.;

Rushabh Amin;

and David Kowalske, Jr.

Subadvisor: Allspring Global Investments, LLC

Category: Tactical allocation

FUND STRATEGY

  • Invests in affiliated mutual funds that collectively provide diversified exposure across and within a range of asset classes and sub-asset classes based on an assessment of changing economic, global market, industry, and issuer conditions
  • Utilizes an active allocation strategy to diversify the portfolio among investments that provide efficient upside returns while managing downside risk across most market cycles with the goal of generating a positive absolute return over an economic cycle

Quarterly review

  • The fund (WARAX) outperformed the Absolute Return Blended Index benchmark in the fourth quarter, driven by strong performance from our overlay and alternative buckets.
  • Growth assets continued to perform strongly in the final quarter of 2025 despite some macroeconomic headwinds. We saw non-U.S. assets continue to gain against U.S. assets and a strong rotation across sectors within the U.S. from which the fund saw benefits.
  • Fixed income markets remained divergent, and our neutral stance toward duration but preference toward curve steepening was an additional contributor to strategy performance.

Market review

Global equities extended third quarter momentum in the fourth quarter, led by emerging markets and developed ex-U.S. The S&P 500 Index gained 2.66%, bringing 2025 returns to 17.88%, while emerging markets, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (Net), surged 33.57%. Sector rotation favored energy and industrials, while consumer discretionary lagged. Fixed income saw modest gains, driven by credit rather than sovereigns. The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was flat at 4.17% after dipping to 3.95% in October. The eurozone and Japan underperformed the benchmark, with yields up 0.14% and 0.36%, respectively. The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index (hedged to USD) was up 0.78%, while the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index was up 1.10%. Credit performed strongly. Precious metals were standout performers—gold soared 65% in 2025, closing at $4,319 (up 12% in the fourth quarter).

Despite a U.S. government shutdown, macroeconomic data signaled resilience. U.S. growth beat expectations, prompting the Federal Reserve to cut rates twice in the fourth quarter amid labor softness. Japan diverged from global easing, hiking rates as gross domestic product rebounded and inflation exceeded target. The eurozone maintained moderate growth, supported by strong labor markets and easing inflation. Emerging markets, especially Latin America, posted robust growth, while China remained in recovery mode with further stimulus expected in March 2026.

Portfolio positioning

OVERALL ASSET ALLOCATION

  • The core portfolio (strategic allocations) was additive to fund performance primarily through exposure to growth assets (equities).
  • We remained positioned with a “pro-risk” stance and continued to prefer non-U.S. versus U.S. assets on a relative basis.

PERFORMANCE DRIVERS

  • Performance was strongly supported by overlay strategies and tactical asset allocation, which were both additive over the quarter.
  • Core fixed income (i.e., duration) was the main detractor over the quarter.

OVERLAYS

  • Within the overlays, our directional strategies fared better than commodity relative value, which struggled amid the strong rally in precious metals.
  • The tactical asset allocation overlay performed well over the quarter, contributing to performance via exposure to curve steepening, precious metals, and non-U.S. over U.S. equities—particularly emerging markets.

ABSOLUTE RETURN FUND EFFECTIVE ALLOCATION (%) AT QUARTER END

Effective allocation is subject to change and may have changed since the date specified. Effective allocation excludes cash, and percent totals may not add to 100% due to rounding.

Portfolio positioning

UNDERLYING FUNDS

  • Fixed income funds generally performed well while equity funds struggled.
  • Alternative risk premia added solidly toward portfolio performance.

OUTLOOK

  • The macroeconomic environment is gradually stabilizing as growth and inflation move into better alignment. Key areas of focus include U.S. labor market trends and potential fiscal developments in China, which could influence our outlook. While inflation is broadly converging toward targets, we see mild upside risks in the U.S.
  • U.S. growth remains supported by fiscal policy and lower rates, despite trade-related headwinds. In contrast, eurozone growth expectations appear optimistic given delays in fiscal stimulus transmission. Fiscal policy globally is expansionary, with China likely to introduce additional measures early in 2026.
  • Policy divergence has continued to shape markets. We expect gradual easing from the Fed and Bank of England, a near-term pause from the European Central Bank, and ongoing tightening by the Bank of Japan.
  • Risk assets should benefit from earnings resilience and supportive policy, underpinning our constructive stance on equities. We have moderated our preference for non-U.S. markets, refining exposures to emerging markets and the eurozone.
  • We remain broadly neutral on credit and duration. Tight spreads limit opportunities in credit while longer-dated developed market bonds remain unattractive amid fiscal credibility concerns.
  • Finally, we are turning moderately constructive on the U.S. dollar, supported by U.S. equity leadership in artificial intelligence-linked sectors, continued fiscal stimulus, and a resilient labor market.

data.title: Ovid Therapeutics: Seizing The Moment With Phase 2 Ready Assets And PIPE Funding
data.description:
Overview

Months following my last evaluation, Ovid Therapeutics' (OVID) lead partnered drug, soticlestat, flunked a Phase 3 trial.

Its stock hasn't yet recovered from that event.

But my focus (even back then) was on Ovid's GABA-AT inhibitor, OV329.

OV329's development hasn't moved much since. I suspect this is why it wasn't able to insulate the stock from the soticlestat failure.

But OVID is showing signs of life after being left for dead.

And, earlier this week, the company released more Phase 1 data, secured funding via a private investment in public equity (PIPE) financing, and outlined the path ahead for OV329.

So now that OV329 is finally in the foreground, I wanted to revisit the stock.

OV329

Ovid is advancing OV329 and OV4071:

OV329, although "Phase 2 ready," is surprisingly still a Phase 1 asset. Ovid plans to initiate a Phase 2 randomized controlled trial in Q2. The lead indication for OV329 will be "drug-resistant adult focal onset seizures."

New data was revealed from the highest tested dose (7 mg). Ovid reports (Slide 13) that "at 7 mg (n=11 healthy volunteers across SAD/MAD), there were no adverse events considered related to study drug."

Ovid points out (so far) that OV329 "does not accumulate in the eye or change human visual health or structure," unlike SABRIL (vigabatrin).

Because the mechanism is already validated in epilepsy, OV329's development is mostly a game of safety and exposure. The end goal is to avoid becoming the "last resort" that SABRIL has effectively come to.

OV329 will still need to prove its ability to reduce seizures. That's what the Phase 2 RCT is for, after all. And given that epilepsy is quite common and has several existing treatments, the path towards the market will be long (that is, time) and narrow (little room for error).

Ovid also recently unveiled its plan to expand OV329 into rare pediatric conditions like TSC and IS. These indications are less crowded and merit commercial perks like orphan pricing.

Pharma DCF

Management did a pretty good job breaking down the TAM "funnel." Some companies will simply reference prevalence to inflate opportunity. But prevalence does not equal TAM.

I seldom "copy and paste" management estimates because they tend to lean optimistic, but I felt management assumptions were reasonable after doing my own research.

So I went with a Target Population of 720k, Net Price of $16k/year, and a 9% market share.

WACC is set to 13% to reflect the high-risk nature of clinical-stage biotech.

Probability of Success was set to 25%. For the typical Phase 2 neurology drug, the POS is actually ~12%. But because the MoA is validated, I leaned optimistic in this estimate.

So we do have a drug that exceeds $1B in peak sales. But because peak sales are a decade from now and POS is low, the risk-adjusted NPV is just $273.4M.

I've included the Pharma DCF cards for IS and TSC in the Appendix.

A few quick notes on IS/TSC assumptions:

  • IS and TSC support orphan drug pricing.
  • Peak share in IS is higher (35%) because TSC (12%) includes EPIDIOLEX and AFINITOR, which are entrenched.
  • POS is higher in TSC (25%) than in IS (18%) because GABA-AT inhibition appears more validated in TSC-related seizure models.

SOTP

Although Ovid ended 2025 with $90.4M in cash and investments, I've also accounted for the $60M PIPE and the $53.9M in gross proceeds expected from warrants expected to be exercised in the coming weeks. So Cash and Shares Out are pro forma author estimates. The PIPE, alongside "Series A warrants," should extend Ovid's cash runway "well into 2029," per management. Ovid "burned" (cash from operations) $37.9M in the trailing twelve months ($12.9M in the last quarter).

My model projects a $385.7M Funding Gap, given the R&D and G&A needs in the next few years before Ovid generates money from OV329. To fill the gap, I assume 11% annual dilution over six years.

The "Current" (technically "pro forma") Market Cap of $519.9M reflects Ovid's market cap with the PIPE and warrants fully accounted for.

The Intrinsic Equity Value of $471.4M (the "raw" value of current assets and obligations before accounting for future dilution) is my projection that adds the Total Global Pipeline value (minus overhead costs) plus cash minus debt ($0).

The Fair Value/Share of $2.16 addresses the Funding Gap by dividing the Total Equity Value (post-raise; fully funded) by the Diluted Share Count.

The Implied Upside (-11.8%) is the difference between the Fair Value/Share and OVID's current price.

Conclusion

Ovid is still in the process of shedding its "soticlestat skin," as its epilepsy pipeline is finally advancing into Phase 2. But there's still a lot of work ahead.

I still do like OV329's prospects in epilepsy. Ovid reminds me a lot of Xenon (XENE). Xenon, too, took a known epilepsy MoA with past safety concerns that derailed market adoption and improved upon safety features.

We also have to be aware of some hurdles ahead. Competition is the most obvious. While Xenon serves as a good comp for Ovid, it's also likely a key competitor. Earlier this month, Xenon's novel KV7 potassium channel opener, azetukalner (XEN1101), impressed in a Phase 3 FOS trial. Xenon plans to submit an NDA in Q3.

And to properly grow into its valuation, which I assess to be pricey, but not ridiculous, Ovid will need quick clinical de-risking.

Despite the risks, I'm willing to keep OVID at a speculative "buy" so long as it quickly advances OV329. Recent developments (e.g., Phase 1 data, PIPE) are supportive of my ongoing speculative "Buy" rating.

Risks

Because OVID is a microcap, I also want to detail some risks that are associated with microcap stocks.

As I alluded to earlier, there is potential for substantial dilution. Without a development partner and revenue, Ovid may have to resort to more dilution to fund OV329 past Phase 2.

Other common microcap-specific risks include volatility, low liquidity, lack of coverage, and high risk of total loss.

Appendix

OV329 in IS

OV329 in TSC

data.title: Build-A-Bear Workshop: Short-Term Noise Masking A Quality Business With +60% Upside Potential
data.description:
Introduction

Build-A-Bear (BBW) has still delivered positive returns in the past year, up close to 17% at the time of writing. Recently, the retailer has seen headwinds that I believe are temporary, but have caused the company to decline close to 37% year-to-date.

However, investors should view this as a buying opportunity, as the current forward P/E of less than 10x is attractive. While I suspect further volatility due to tariffs, overall market volatility, and rising recession risks, I see share price expansion in the medium term.

In this article, I discuss BBW's latest earnings, what caused their stock to pull back, and why long-term investors should view this as a buying opportunity.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Build-A-Bear in December 2025 after the stock saw its share price pullback from its 52-week high of nearly $76 to roughly $53 a share.

Since then, their underperformance has continued, with the retailer down an additional 26.75%. This is significantly more than the S&P (SP500), down close to 2% at the time of writing.

During Q3, results were mixed with a beat by $0.03 on their bottom line and a $1.58 million miss on revenue. Both were $0.62 and $122.7 million, respectively. Both also declined from the prior quarter's $0.94 and $124.2 million.

At the time, BBW was impacted by lower pre-tax income, net retail sales, and e-commerce demand. Still, I remained high conviction over the long term. And their more than 60% upside to their price target of $80 supported my thesis.

Tariffs, Margins, Underperformance

Although the market has experienced heightened volatility in recent weeks due to the ongoing war, Build-A-Bear's underperformance has been validated by their share price falling more than 48% in the past 6 months.

During the quarter, the retailer showed resilience, but headwinds were very evident. Again, results were mixed, similar to Q3 with a slight beat on EPS and a miss on their top line.

EPS amounted to $1.26, beating by $0.04, while revenue came in below estimates at $154.5 million. Revenue missed by a slightly lower amount than Q3 at $1.21 million.

BBW's results were due to the sequential decline in pre-tax income, flat net retail sales, and lower e-commerce demand. In recent months, BBW has seen a significant impact from tariffs.

Pre-tax income saw a $16 million impact, while their bottom line saw a $0.65 headwind. For the quarter, pre-tax income was down $6 million year-over-year to $21.5 million.

EPS was down from $1.62 the prior year's quarter. For the year, pre-tax income saw a slight increase of $0.1 million to $67.2 million. Their bottom line showed solid growth, up 5.8% from $3.77 to $3.99. Revenue increased 6.7% from $496.4 million to $529.8 million.

Besides tariffs, BBW also saw severe weather impact the company, causing select store closures, digital headwinds, and elevated inventory. Forced store closures caused a $2 million impact on revenue, while inventory increased $12.4 million.

Traffic was down approximately 1% compared to the prior year's quarter that saw traffic increase 4%. E-commerce demand was down a sizable 13.6% for the quarter and 5.5% for the year.

As a result, gross margins were down 140 basis points to 55.2%, partially offset by price increases. So, while BBW's headwinds reveal ongoing issues, the company showed resilience with its 5th consecutive year of record revenues and pre-tax income.

2026 To See More Headwinds

During earnings, management stated they expect further impacts from tariffs, but that revenue should accelerate in the back half of the year. They're expecting $8 million in incremental tariff costs in the first half.

And while the Supreme Court struck down the President's tariffs, BBW's management stated they didn't have a date on refunds. Another indicator of headwinds is pre-tax income is expected to be down in the mid- to single-digits on the low end.

This reflects a full year's worth of tariffs, expected to impact the company by $16 million. On the high end, growth is expected to be in the low, single digits. Currently, earnings are projected to decline modestly to $3.86 from $3.99.

In their Commercial segment, they're expecting at least 20% growth, mostly in the back half. Revenue is expected to grow mid-single digits.

Expansion Remains A Key Driver Of Growth

During the quarter, BBW added 11 new locations across 4 continents, bringing the total to 64. For 2025, they expanded into 8 new countries, slightly down from 10 in 2024. They ended the year with a total of 662 locations. The past two years, BBW has expanded into 36 new countries.

I personally think expansion, particularly in high-growth tourist areas & international markets, will benefit the company long-term. Both should continue to be solid growth drivers going forward.

During the quarter, they opened two new locations, one in the Mall of America and the American Dream Mall, near the New York City metropolitan area. For 2026, they expect to open 50 new locations, mostly partner-operated.

Furthermore, they partnered with Walmart (WMT) for the first time, launching 2 limited collections. These are expected to be rolled out next week through mid-May. This should also help sales in the coming quarters.

Clean Balance Sheet, Dividend, & Buybacks

Build-A-Bear's most attractive metric is their clean balance sheet with no debt and $26.8 million in cash. This allows them to focus on expansion, frequent share repurchases, and dividend increases. They recently announced a penny increase to the dividend, making it $0.23 a share.

This was a lower increase than last year's 10% and is further evidence of persistent headwinds. For the year, BBW returned $39 million to shareholders through buybacks and dividends; $11.5 million were in dividends.

Management took more than half of a million shares off the table, buying back a total of 508.9 million for the year. For the quarter, they bought back 172,904 shares for $10.1 million.

Subsequent to the quarter's end, they took advantage of the share price, buying back an additional 132,230 shares, and had $55 million remaining on their current program.

Compelling Upside, But There Are Risks

Aside from their debt-free balance sheet and frequent buybacks, Build-A-Bear also offers significant upside to their price target of $62.

While this has declined from $80 previously, this still gives investors more than 60% price appreciation potential. Throw in the low forward P/E of less than 10x and a decent yield above 2%, Build-A-Bear's investment case is compelling.

Going forward, I expect the company to remain volatile and trade in the mid-$30s to mid-$40 range. One, due to persistent headwinds, and two, because of deteriorating sentiment. With the war ongoing, it's likely to see volatility increase should it go on longer.

The reason being is a longer war, in my opinion, increases the risk of a recession. And if this happens, this will also impact BBW's sales and margins. Recently, recession odds jumped on Kalshi. And the longer the war rages on, the higher I believe the odds will go.

And this will likely result in Build-A-Bear seeing increased volatility since a recession would impact consumers' discretionary income, leading to a potential decline in financials for the retailer.

Conclusion

While Build-A-Bear is likely to see persistent headwinds carry over into 2026, investors should view the pullback from their 52-week high as a buying opportunity.

I prefer to buy shares below $40 because of my low-cost basis and recently added to my position as a result. If it continues to fall, I will look to add more in the mid to low $30s.

If we do see a recession, I suspect shares will decline further and potentially hit a new 52-week low. This is because traffic will likely decline, impacting sales and margins.

But the retailer's debt-free balance sheet and rapid expansion give me confidence that they can see solid upside once we see more economic clarity, likely over the next 12 to 24 months.

data.title: HelloFresh: Efficiency Gains Mask A Business Without A Growth Engine
data.description:
I last covered HelloFresh SE (HLFFF, HELFY) in July 2024, arguing the company faced a fundamental scalability issue. My thesis was that HelloFresh relied on generous marketing expenses to grow their user base but failed to convert customers to long-term users at a satisfying rate. I bet that, long term, the company would have either managed to fix this structural issue or pivoted to a new business model.

Today, almost two years after my first coverage, I am reviewing HelloFresh again to assess whether their business model is sustainable and an investment case can be made.

A flawed business model in two charts

My bearish thesis for HelloFresh is fairly simple: the company relies on generous handouts to acquire new customers and retain existing ones, but customers do not actually remain loyal to the company to a satisfying degree (i.e., the churn rate is too high).

The two charts below, which I updated from my 2024 coverage, show marketing expenses and operating income for HelloFresh on an annual basis. At that time, based on 2023 data, I argued what follows:

The trend, in my opinion, is clear: while HelloFresh is indeed managing to scale its business, it does so primarily via handouts to acquire and retain customers. The company almost tripled in revenue since 2020, but it has done so by disproportionately increasing its marketing expenses. As a result, HelloFresh has been unable to steadily grow its Operating Income.

Two years later, the picture is bittersweet and somewhat bearish.

On the positive side, HelloFresh did manage to slow down their marketing expenses, which have stabilized at around ~€1.4 billion for the past three years.

But at what cost?

The company's revenue (not shown in these charts) has also stalled at about ~€7.9 billion (slightly decreasing since its 2023 peak).

More worryingly, operating income has also stalled, and the company has been roughly breaking even in both 2024 and 2025.

In other terms, the company stopped growing their customer base (and revenue) as soon as their marketing spend stalled, proving my original point.

I see this situation as proof the business model of HelloFresh is fundamentally flawed long term. Customers are simply not sticking with a meal-kit subscription model without being given handouts, or not at a pace that makes such a business sustainable long term.

The consumer insight behind a flawed business model: convenience

From a consumer standpoint, the idea that a meal kit service is not financially sustainable has some relatively straightforward grounds.

People are generally willing to pay for convenience when it comes to food and food delivery. Businesses like DoorDash, Inc. (DASH), have been able to scale back their marketing expenses and climb to profitability over the years. They managed to "hook" people on the convenience of having food delivered to their door, even well after the pandemic made this a necessity.

By contrast, delivering meal kits that still need to be cooked at home significantly decreases the convenience element. It is then little surprise that consumers are unwilling to stay with such a subscription model long term.

A flawed business warranted a pivot, turning out to be worse than the original

A flawed business model per se would not warrant a bearish thesis on HelloFresh. Any company with good enough management can successfully recover from a weak core business by pivoting to a more profitable one.

In this regard, I find it very indicative how Blue Apron, the second-largest meal-kit delivery service in the US, recently pivoted their business model. The company is moving from subscriptions to on-demand orders of meal kits, further adding two new product lines that directly address the convenience consumer element:

  • "Dish by Blue Apron," consisting of ready-to-eat, nutrition-focused meals that only need to be reheated.

  • "Assemble & Bake," which are meal kits coming together all on one sheet pan (significantly reducing the "cooking" element).

HelloFresh also followed a similar pivot, pushing RTE (ready-to-eat meals), a product line acquired in 2020 under the brand "Factor". However, this pivot has so far been a disappointment, with management mentioning in the most recent earnings call:

In [RTE], we absorbed the consequences of regulatory-driven food manufacturing challenges that cascaded into product quality issues and impacted customer experience at exactly the wrong time, while we were simultaneously spending on brand and performance marketing. The result was worse unit economics, lower marketing ROI and lower customer retention, resulting in a significant gap to our original plan.

In fact, HelloFresh's sluggish performance in 2025 was mostly driven by the RTE (ready-to-eat) segment, with a full-year RTE AEBITDA margin at -1.2%.

Blue Apron (which is part of Wonder Group) is not listed anymore, and there is limited data available for this company. Therefore, we cannot possibly know whether their own pivot was more successful from a financial standpoint.

Ultimately what matters in the context of my thesis is that finding new, more profitable ventures seems a necessity for all companies in this industry.

Management talks about an 'efficiency reset': is this enough?

Given the secular weakness of "meal-kit-as-a-service" and the failure of RTE, the big question is what is management doing to address their profitability issues?

In the latest earnings reports available, management mentions "efficiency resets" as a key focus area. The narrative they are proposing is that of a cyclical business that is undergoing a stagnation phase (with weak demand). Management expects demand to turn positive again in "18 to 20 months" and mentions they are focusing on efficiencies in the meantime (see chart below).

And efficiencies have indeed been delivered. For the full year 2025, HelloFresh marketing spend was cut by over €203 million versus 2024. In meal kits specifically, the year-over-year percentage reduction in marketing spend was double the rate of order decline. US meal kit marketing spend fell more than 40% in USD terms in 2025. This, together with other efficiency measures, contributed to making the core business of meal kits financially sustainable.

However, I will be honest and say I am not reassured by this overall narrative in the slightest. Even if I were to believe that meal kits are going to come back in fashion (which I do not), we are talking about what today is a declining industry. And this is acknowledged by HelloFresh, as shown in the slide below. Overall, meal kits have been in decline, while a core base of "tenured" customers has remained highly stable. Clearly, though, this base has not been enough to bring the company where it needs to be in terms of profitability.

The reason management is focusing on meal kits is fairly easy to understand: the pivot to RTE has not worked and ended up hindering the company's financial performance. But this, in my view, should not be a reason to double down on a legacy business that has been stagnant for a couple of years. Even if the core business may be profitable in itself, I do not think investing in a slowly decaying business that depends on efficiencies to operate can ever constitute a compelling investment case.

In this regard, I really think that HelloFresh needs to either show that RTE can be brought to growth or find yet another business to pivot to.

Why a 'Hold': HelloFresh would trade at ~8x P/E without RTE

In writing this article, I hesitated between a SELL and a HOLD rating until the last moment. On the one hand, this is a barely profitable business that has seen declining revenues for three years now. Management has also failed in their pivot to RTE, and they are somewhat doubling down on their meal kits again, providing questionable guidance for a return to growth in 2027.

However, I ultimately decided that a HOLD rating is more appropriate. The reason is that if we strip down the RTE business from HelloFresh, the underlying business is profitable and very cheaply valued.

HelloFresh reports AEBITDA margins at the segment level in the deck, which allows it to back-calculate revenues precisely. The table below extrapolates data for the meal kit business from financial figures reported in euros.

Data in Million Euros

Assumptions for the above extrapolation are as follows:

  • Holding costs and "others" fully allocated to meal kits (conservative, since some would disappear without RTE complexity)

  • D&A allocated proportionally (~69% of group's total)

  • SBC kept in full (concentrated in holding)

  • Net interest proportionally adjusted

At its current market capitalization of ~€600 million (and €3.78 per share on the German stock exchange), the hypothetical valuation of HelloFresh if it only sold meal kits would be at a ~7.7X P/E. A fair valuation, in my view, warrants a HOLD.

Further risks and considerations for investors

HelloFresh's business is strictly tied to consumer sentiment. A future US and/or global recession may hinder management's idea that meal-kit demand should go back to growth in 18 to 20 months. It may also permanently depress long-term demand for a business that, for the past few years, has proven not to be sticky in terms of consumer habits. What worked in favor of the company during the pandemic may turn into a secular tailwind in case of a recession.

On the other side, if macro conditions improve quickly, the bottom for the stock may already be in as the business re-accelerates towards sustained profitability.

In this sense, investors should be aware that HelloFresh is a company that is fairly sensitive to changes in macroeconomic conditions.

Finally, it remains to be seen what direction management is going to take in the future months. Should they decide to enter a new vertical with promising growth potential and executional excellence, a BUY rating may be warranted.

Conclusion: HelloFresh cannot focus on meal kits long-term

Despite failing to grow its bottom or top line, HelloFresh at least managed to make its core business of meal-kit delivery profitable. The company did fail to execute a pivot to RTE, but management now has more breathing space to figure out their next move.

For a long-term investment case, I think we will need to see either of the following:

  • A successful turnaround of the RTE business, to the point this can be seen as a credible, future lever for growth.

  • The company entering a new adjacent space or industry altogether and either scrapping their RTE plans or bringing them (at minimum) to break-even.

Overall, I am not convinced yet by what I saw during the latest earnings. Management so far seems to want to double down on meal-kit delivery. But focusing on a declining meal-kit business that centers on "tenured customers" at the expense of volume cannot warrant a BUY case.

The market's reaction after earnings speaks for itself: the company experienced a steep sell-off after earnings, and it is now down ~40% YTD and ~95% from its all-time high.

For investors having HelloFresh in their portfolios, I think a 'Hold' can make sense to see whether the company can successfully reinvent itself, given the core business is profitable. Others may want to stay out of HelloFresh for the time being.

data.title: USD Comes Back Bid After Yesterday's Exaggerated Slide
data.description:
The market seemed to overreact to the central bank meetings this week. The market heard Fed Chair Powell as more hawkish than the FOMC statement and took the dollar sharply higher. Yesterday, it overreacted to the Bank of England and European Central Banks and sold the greenback aggressively. The swaps market is discounting three rate hikes this year by the ECB and BOE and about three basis points of tightening by the Federal Reserve.

Still, after yesterday’s sell-off, the dollar has bounced back. The fog of war seems to contribute to the desire for short-term market participants not wanting to be short dollars into the weekend. Even though the US and Israel say that they will not strike Iranian oil infrastructure, there is little sign of de-escalation, and yesterday, US Treasury Secretary Bessent made a reference to the possibility that Kharg Island could be taken over by the US. Adding to the mix is today’s “triple-witching” that see a relatively large $5.7 trillion of options on individual stocks, indices and exchange-traded funds expire. This comes amid a further sell-off in stocks and bonds.

Prices

G10

• After practically no follow-through euro selling yesterday and the sharp drop during Fed Chair Powell’s press conference on Wednesday, the euro rallied strongly yesterday. It reached a six-session high, almost $1.1615. ECB President Lagarde seemed to take a wait-and-see stance and expressed no sense of urgency, but unnamed hawks played up the risk of a hike as early as next month, and today the Bundesbank’s chief made the same point. And it is not just one hike, but now the swaps are pricing in nearly three hikes before the end of the year. However, with the weekend at hand, given the fog of war, the short-term market does not want to be short dollars. The euro has held below $1.16 today and was sold in the $1.1535 area ahead of the North American session. This risks a test on $1.15, where options for 1.5 bln euros expire today.

• The yen rose to a seven-session high yesterday as the greenback unwound the Powell-inspired gains. The dollar rose to within a whisker of JPY160 on Wednesday and early Thursday before being sold to JPY157.50 in the North American afternoon. The 20-day moving average, which the dollar has not traded below since February 24 (~JPY157.75 today), was frayed. However, market seemed to overreact yesterday, and with Tokyo markets closed for the national holiday, the greenback recovered to around JPY158.90 today. There are $920 mln options at JPY158.50 that expire today.

• Sterling also recovered smartly yesterday. After it slipped below $1.3250 in the early going, it poked above $1.3465, its best level since March 10. It took out the 20-day moving average (~$1.3405) for the first time since the middle of last month on a set of five-session highs that frayed the 20-day moving average (~$1.3400 today). There was no follow-through sterling buying today, and it mostly held below $1.3440 so far today. Nearby support is around $1.3345-55. The swaps market swung from practically flat Wednesday to pricing in an 80% chance of a hike by the Bank of England next month. Before the war, the market had a cut in April fully discounted. Three hikes before the end of the year are fully discounted.

• The Canadian dollar was the weakest currency in the G10 yesterday. It was practically flat. It was the first session since late January that the US dollar (DXY) did not trade below CAD1.3700. It is holding above CAD1.37 so far today in quiet turnover. While the greenback made a marginal new two-and-a-half-week high yesterday, it stalled near CAD1.3750, while the month’s high was a little above it. It is not clear that the consolidation pattern has ended. There are nearly $1 bln roughly divided between three strikes, CAD1.3725, CAD1.3740, and CAD1.3750 expiring today.

• The Australian dollar held $0.7000 and recovered to almost nearly $0.7110 in the North American afternoon yesterday. Tuesday and Wednesday’s highs were around $0.7120-25. It is trading a bit heavier today and has been sold to session lows, slightly below $0.7055 in Europe. Nearby support is seen in the $0.7020-40 area.

EM

• Despite the risk-off mood, the Mexican peso recovered from a four-day low to settle higher on the day. The US dollar reached almost MXN17.96 before being sold slightly below MXN17.68. It has mostly traded between MXN17.50 and MXN18.00 this month and settled a little north of the midpoint. The greenback is trading with a firmer bias and has approached MXN17.83 in Europe. The Brazilian real gained slightly more than the Mexican peso yesterday (~0.85 vs. ~0.65). The two led Latam currencies higher yesterday.

• The dollar turned lower after reaching a three-day high against the offshore yuan, near CNH6.9076 yesterday. As the greenback was sold more broadly, it fell to new session lows around CNH6.8870 in the North American afternoon. It is trading inside yesterday’s range. It settled last week near CNH6.9065. We had thought the dollar fix might be raised this week, but instead, the PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate at a new multi-year low today (CNY6.8898 vs. CNY6.8975 yesterday and CNY6.9007 at the end of last week).

• Indian rupee was sold aggressively. The rupee fell by as much as 1.2% today, the most since February 2022. Foreign selling of assets took a toll and lifted the dollar to a record INR93.76. The INR95 area may be tested next week.

Other Markets

• Asia Pacific equities were mostly lower, and Tokyo markets were on holiday. South Korea’s Kospi bucked the trend and posted a small gain, as have the Indian indices. The Stoxx 600 (STOXX) in Europe is little changed after it fell 2.4% yesterday. US index futures point to a lower opening. The S&P 500 (SPX) settled below its 200-day moving average yesterday for the first time since May 2025.

• Bonds also are under pressure. Benchmark 10-year yields are 4-7 bp higher in Europe, led by the UK Gilts and Italian BTPs. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is up five basis points to 4.30%. The high yield was around 4.32% yesterday, the highest since last August.

• Gold is little changed as it consolidates after the ~$365 loss over the past two sessions. Silver is trading heavier, and it could be the fourth consecutive session of losses of more than $1 an ounce.

• May WTI has been confined to the lower end of yesterday’s range but is near session highs, above $96, ahead of the start of the North American session. Yesterday’s push above $100 briefly marks the week’s high. The May-July Brent futures contracts remain above $100.

Data

• Canada reports January retail sales today. Based on preliminary data, StatsCan tipped a sharp 1.5% gain after a 0.4% decline in December. Excluding auto sales, a 1.2% jump is projected by the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey after a 0.1% rise at the end of last year.

• The UK reported February government finances today, and they were worse than expected. Still, the surge in interest rates renders the data moot. The UK 10-year Gilt yield is up 70 bp since the war began, and the two-year Gilt yield has risen by about 100 bp.

• The eurozone reported January’s trade and current account surpluses of 12.1 bln and 37.9 bln euros, respectively. It is driven by Germany, of course. It is notable that the IMF estimates that Germany’s current account surplus was 5.8% of GDP in 2024 and 4.5% last year. It is projected to rise to 5.1% this year. The IMF estimates that China’s current account surplus was 2.3% of GDP in 2024 and 3.8% in 2025. It forecasts a 2.8% surplus this year. There are critics who say that China’s surplus is larger than the IMF says, but while many IMF projections are debated by private sector economists, they offer a consistent baseline view.

• As widely anticipated, Chinese banks left their one- and five-year loan prime rates steady at 3.0% and 3.5%, respectively. They were last changed in May 2025, when they were lowered by 10 bp.

data.title: Yield Of Dreams: A High-Yield Dividend Retirement Portfolio Without The Nightmares
data.description:
When building a portfolio to live off of dividends in retirement, chasing yields seldom works, as it often steers investors into the path of some major landmines that end up blowing up via dividend cuts and major stock price declines. That being said, it is very possible to still build a portfolio of high-dividend stocks that prove to be dependable over time.

However, I do not necessarily advise it for everyone, because in order to do this effectively, you really need to be a capable and willing active investor. High-yielding stocks are generally not the type that you buy and hold indefinitely, but instead, you need to actively monitor and actively manage your portfolio of them. Additionally, I only suggest trying to maximize your retirement income through almost exclusively high-dividend stocks if you absolutely need to generate enough income to retire on. If you have a really large portfolio and can easily live on a dividend yield of 3%, 4%, or 5%, I would personally target a more conservative portfolio, especially if you do not want to be an active investor. If you want to be an active investor in high-yield dividend stocks, and you need to feel comfortable evaluating and managing them, by all means, do so, regardless of your portfolio size.

If you are instead looking for more of a buy-and-hold approach, I think it would be better to focus on some lower-yielding, more sustainable stocks like Realty Income (O), Dividend Kings and Dividend Aristocrats (REGL) (NOBL) like Coca-Cola (KO) and Procter & Gamble (PG), as well as broadly diversified low-cost dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) that has a dividend yield of about 3.5%. However, for those who are pursuing a high-yielding dividend portfolio, I want to provide a few guidelines and individual investment ideas in this article to help you on your journey.

Four Pillars of High-Yield Success

First and foremost, it is important to apply the following four principles when deciding if a stock should belong in a high-yielding portfolio for retirement income:

  1. It needs to generate sufficiently high income so that you can meet your expenses and/or conform to your high-yielding investing strategy. What exactly that number is clearly depends on the individual's security and your personal circumstances.
  2. It must be backed by a durable and defensive business model, and it fits in with your other positions in your portfolio so that you have a well-diversified portfolio across individual sectors.
  3. It must be backed by a solid balance sheet and a reasonably conservative payout ratio such that the dividend appears to be on a sustainable footing.
  4. Finally, the dividend income stream needs to be able to keep up with inflation over time, so it either needs to be generating underlying dividend growth per share and/or generating sufficient surplus income that you can reinvest a portion of it to grow your dividend income by buying more shares over time.

Diversification Beyond Traditional Stocks

With these principles in mind, what are some good elements of a high-yielding dividend portfolio? Well, back to the idea that you need sufficient diversification and durable defensive business models, I think it is important to make sure that you are holding a fairly wide variety of securities from a fairly wide variety of sectors. Fortunately, this can be done while still generating high yields.

For example, you can invest in baby bonds and even some low-investment-grade or high-yield corporate bonds (HYG) that can help to boost your income while being fairly conservative. If you can diversify effectively or even invest in a bond fund, this could serve you quite well. The same applies for preferreds (PFF), where you can get some pretty quality preferreds or even preferred ETFs.

Additional sectors are the business development company space, where you can get high single-digit to low double-digit yields that should be fairly sustainable if you are effective in your selection and active in your portfolio management. Then, of course, there are midstream master limited partnerships, especially if you do not mind dealing with a K-1, or alternatively, you could buy CEFs and ETFs from the sector that can give you a high single-digit or even a low double-digit yield.

You can also buy real estate investment trusts, some of which can yield fairly high amounts of dividends, or even some CEFs and ETFs that have attractive yields. Infrastructure (IFRA) is another sector where you can find some attractive and sustainable yields both through individual stock picks and through funds. Alternative asset managers are not typically high-yielding, but these days, since they are out of favor, you can definitely buy some high-yielding ones, and they combine fairly durable dividends that are backed by long-dated fee-based income streams with attractive growth potential over the long term, especially if they can overcome their current market bearish sentiment.

Additionally, covered call ETFs (JEPI), if used prudently, can be valuable portfolio diversifiers that also generate significant income. Finally, certain precious metals (GLD) leases and ETFs can also generate attractive income and further diversify a portfolio.

Top Picks for Sustainable High Income

As for specific names from these sectors, there are some pretty attractive baby bonds in the BDC space, like the Trinity Capital (TRIN) baby bonds (TRINI), along with several others in that space and beyond, which should be quite sustainable and offer attractive high single-digit yields. Alternatively, you can buy actively managed bond funds, like the Infrastructure Capital Bond Income ETF (BNDS), that yield 8% on a trailing 12-month basis, pay dividends monthly, are actively managed, and have grown their dividends slowly over time, making an attractive combination of high yield and at least some income growth.

In the preferred space, you can also buy attractive individual preferreds, including names like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners preferreds (BIP.PR.A) (BIP.PR.B), among several others, or you can buy actively managed preferred funds, like the Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA). It is similar to BNDS except for preferreds. It is actively managed and, in this case, uses a bit of leverage. PFFA also pays dividends monthly and has a history of growing its dividends at a slight rate each year. Thanks to its leverage and higher risk profile than BNDS due to investing in preferreds, PFFA currently yields 9.8% on a trailing 12-month basis.

In the BDC space, you can buy a passively managed ETF like the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) and an actively managed ETF like the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC), or you can buy blue-chip BDCs at attractive valuations, such as Ares Capital (ARCC), which today yields 10.5%, or Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which today yields 12.7%. If you want to be a little bit less aggressive, you can invest in a name like Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) that has a mid-single-digit base dividend yield, which extends to around 7.8% if you include supplemental dividends.

In the midstream MLP space, there are proven names like Energy Transfer (ET) and MPLX (MPLX) that provide attractive yields north of 7% that should grow at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time while also providing tax-deferred income. Alternatively, you can invest in ETFs like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), the GlobalX MLP ETF (MLPA), or the NEOS MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI), all of which pay out high yields and give K-1-free diversification within the sector.

In the REIT space, it is a bit harder to find a high yield, as the standard passively managed ETF, Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), only yields 3.8% on a trailing 12-month basis. However, if you invest in funds like the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), you can get monthly payouts, a track record of outperformance over the long term, and an 8.6% dividend yield. Meanwhile, in the infrastructure space, some of my favorite funds include the Reeves Utility Income Fund (UTG) and the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), both of which have mid- to high single-digit yields with monthly payouts and well-diversified portfolios, as well as fairly attractive long-term total return track records.

Individual names in the space that I like include Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) (BIPC), Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) (BEPC), and Clearway Energy (CWEN) (CWEN.A), which offer mid-single-digit yields and attractive dividend growth profiles.

The alternative asset management space has pulled back sharply recently, which has created a plethora of attractive opportunities, including names like Blackstone (BX) and Ares Management (ARES), both of which yield north of 5%, as well as others in the sector that also offer attractive yields.

Finally, when it comes to covered call ETFs, it is important to know exactly why you are adding them. I, personally, think that the most useful reason to hold them is for diversification to sectors that normally do not generate much income at all. For example, mega-cap tech is a large and dynamic sector that generally does not pay out much in the way of dividends. If you invest in a fund like the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), or the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), you can get some pretty attractive income paid monthly while still having underlying exposure to the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ).

Another sector that might be worth doing covered calls with is precious metals via funds like the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI). However, I think an even better way to generate income from precious metals is through investing in a diversified pool of leases where you fully own the underlying bullion without the upside being capped at all. You can earn a mid-single-digit yield on that lease investment as well.

Investor Takeaway

This strategy is not risk-free at all and does require active management, as I say at the beginning, because some of these individual holdings may begin to run into trouble or sector allocations may end up out of alignment based on market volatility. Regular monitoring and active management of a portfolio like this are particularly useful.

Additionally, if you have a high-holding stock that appreciates a lot and another stock that becomes heavily discounted, capital recycling driven by valuations and fundamentals can be a great way to enhance returns and income growth over time. In fact, this is a strategy that I think is particularly well-suited to high-yield portfolios, as I detailed here.

Overall, however, if you can successfully diversify and head off potential dividend cuts when problems first emerge, this can be an extremely rewarding strategy for any investor, but especially retirees looking to live off of dividends. You can generate a lot more passive income from your investments than you would be able to by simply investing in a passive dividend-paying ETF, while still being able to keep the risk of dividend cuts and long-term total return on underperformance at bay by using effective portfolio management strategies. This is exactly how we run our portfolios that yield between 7 and 8% and have significantly outperformed over the long term while having below-market beta at High Yield Investor.

data.title: U.S. Lower And Middle Classes Shrinking Over Time As More Households Earn Higher Incomes
data.description:
The ranks of lower- and middle-class households in the United States are thinning. The reason why is remarkable: more households are earning higher incomes, allowing them to move up into the top ranks of the nation's income spectrum.

You don't have to take our word for it. We've organized the U.S. Census Bureau's inflation-adjusted data for household income from 1967 through 2024 into three groups. The first group contains households with annual total money income of $49,999 or less, which represents lower income-earning households. The second group contains households earning between $50,000 and $149,999 to represent middle income-earning households. The third group contains all households earning $150,000 or more.

The following chart confirms the percentage share of lower- and middle-class households in the U.S. is shrinking as the percentage of upper-class households increases.

U.S. households earning $150,000 or more in inflation-adjusted constant 2024 U.S. dollars have risen from 4.6% of all households to 26.1% from 1967 through 2024. Middle-ranked households earning between $50,000 and $149,999 has fallen from 52.4% to 43.8% of all U.S. households. The lowest-ranked households earning real incomes of $49,999 or less has plunged from accounting for 43.0% of all U.S. households to just 30.2%.

A similar pattern holds for U.S. families. See more commentary on this phenomenon here and here.

Reference

U.S. Census Bureau. Historical Income Tables: Households. Table H-17. Households by Total Money Income, Race, and Hispanic Origin of Householder. [Excel spreadsheet]. 25 August 2025.

data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures rise as Netanyahu eases concerns about the Iran conflict. (00:15) Super Micro (SMCI) falls as co-founder, employee charged in Nvidia chip smuggling case. (00:48)Unilever (UL) in talks to sell its food business to McCormick (MKC). (03:00)

This is an abridged transcript.

Stock index futures are firmly in the green.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) rose 0.90%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) gained 1.02%, and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures (INDU) advanced 0.97%.

Bitcoin is up 1% at $70,000. Gold is up 0.5% at $4,677.

Market sentiment improved after Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu said the country is helping to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Oil prices slipped on the news that Netanyahu said Iran no longer has the capacity to enrich uranium or make ballistic missiles.

Crude oil is down 0.2% at $95. Brent crude is at $109.

European indexes also rebound after an ease in oil prices.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.2% and the DAX is up 0.6%.

In Asia, the markets in Japan (NKY:IND) closed for a holiday.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is down 22% in premarket action.

Three individuals linked to the AI server maker, including a co-founder, were charged with violating export laws by assisting in the smuggling of at least $2.5 billion worth of U.S. AI technology to China.

The Justice Department did not name Super ‌Micro in the complaint, referring only to a "U.S. manufacturer."

The company said it was informed by federal prosecutors of the indictment on Thursday. It noted that it was not named as a defendant in the case and said it had cooperated with investigators.

In an indictment unsealed on Thursday, the U.S. government alleged that Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Ruei-Tsan “Steven” Chang and Ting-Wei “Willy” Sun worked together to violate the Export Control Reform Act. Liaw co-founded Super ​Micro in 1993, and joined its board of directors in 2023. Chang was a sales manager in the Taiwan office of Super Micro, while Sun was ⁠a contractor.

U.S. officials allege the trio went to great lengths to hide their actions from both U.S.-based server manufacturers and export control authorities, even using hair dryers to remove labels and serial numbers ​from the real machines and placing them on dummy machines left behind after the real machines had been shipped to China.

The efforts have yielded around $2.5 billion in sales for the server maker since 2024, with $510 million sold between late April 2025 and mid-May 2025 going to the Southeast Asian company and on to China, the indictment said. The plaintiff said the server maker had no U.S. Commerce Department license to export servers featuring Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to China.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) said it placed its co-founder and the sales manager on leave and terminated its ties with the contractor, after being made aware of the charges on Thursday.

The attorney’s office said the co-founder and contractor were both arrested on Thursday, while the sales manager is a fugitive.

Unilever (UL) announced that it received an inbound offer for its food business and is in discussions with McCormick (MKC).

The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter, that an all-stock deal may be announced within weeks if talks don't fall apart. The exact structure remains unknown.

Unilever's (UL) food business, which houses brands such as Knorr and Hellmann's, could be worth tens of billions of dollars. McCormick's (MKC) products include Frank's RedHot sauce and French's yellow mustard.

Separating its food business would enable Unilever (UL) to focus on its beauty, personal care and home divisions. The company spun off its ice cream business Magnum last year.
